title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a community repair cafe. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Nia, The Phone Fixer
Nia joined the monthly repair cafe after noticing that neighbours were throwing away phones with cracked screens or weak batteries. She had repaired devices for friends at university, but the cafe was her first time explaining delicate work to strangers under time pressure. [47]Before touching anyone's phone, she insisted on photographing its condition and writing down exactly what the owner had asked her to attempt.[/47] Some visitors found the procedure excessive, until it prevented a dispute about an existing scratch. Nia enjoyed the practical challenge, but what changed her attitude was meeting people who kept old devices because they contained messages from relatives. [48]She says the project taught her that repair is often emotional, not merely economical.[/48] She also learned to refuse jobs politely when replacement parts were unsafe or clearly counterfeit. By the end of the year, she had trained two teenagers to handle basic battery changes under supervision.
texts.B:
### Graham, The Sewing Specialist
Graham brought a portable sewing machine to the cafe after retiring from costume work at a theatre. [49]He was surprised that the busiest hour came just before closing, when people finally found the courage to admit they did not know how to thread a needle.[/49] Instead of repairing clothes silently, he made each owner operate the foot pedal for at least one seam. He believed confidence mattered more than a perfect result, especially for students trying to make uniforms last longer on very little money. [50]His background in theatre helped him improvise strong but invisible patches when fabric was too worn for ordinary stitching.[/50] The only part he disliked was explaining that some cheap zips cost more to replace than the trousers were worth. Still, he kept a box of rescued buttons arranged by colour, and regular visitors began bringing donations for it, along with short stories about where each button came from.
texts.C:
### Priya, The Tool Librarian
Priya was responsible for the cupboard of shared tools, which sounded simple until she discovered how often screwdrivers vanished into pockets during busy afternoon sessions. [51]She introduced a numbered token system so that every borrowed tool could be matched to the person using it.[/51] The system looked old-fashioned, but losses dropped immediately. Priya had no specialist repair skill, so she became an expert in pairing visitors with the right volunteer and calming the queue when sessions ran behind or a repair proved impossible. [52]Her most awkward moment was asking a local councillor to wait his turn after he tried to move ahead of everyone else.[/52] Later he apologised and funded new work lamps, which she accepted with a smile. Priya says the cafe works because nobody is treated as a customer; everyone is expected to learn something before leaving, even if the object cannot be saved that particular difficult day.
texts.D:
### Leo, The Radio Restorer
Leo came to the cafe carrying a wooden radio he had restored for his grandfather, and someone immediately asked him to volunteer. [53]He agreed only after being promised a quiet corner, because he found the crowded hall overwhelming during his first visit.[/53] Old radios suited him: their circuits were visible, their faults logical, and their owners usually patient, especially once he explained each step carefully before touching the fragile case. [54]The most memorable repair involved replacing a tiny corroded wire while the owner told him the same radio had broadcast her wedding announcement decades earlier.[/54] Leo admits he once broke a dial by rushing, so he now stops work whenever he feels watched or hurried. [55]He keeps a notebook of unusual faults because the same problem often returns months later in a different model.[/55] [56]For him, the best result is not applause, but hearing repaired radios playing softly on the refreshment table.[/56]

questions.question: mentions recording the original state of an item before beginning work on it?
questions.explanation: Nia photographed each phone and wrote down what the owner wanted before attempting the repair.

questions.question: says the activity revealed a sentimental reason why people wanted objects repaired?
questions.explanation: Nia learned that repair was often emotional because devices contained messages from relatives.

questions.question: was surprised by when people most often admitted lacking a basic skill?
questions.explanation: Graham says the busiest hour came just before closing, when people admitted they could not thread a needle.

questions.question: used experience from a previous creative job to solve repair problems?
questions.explanation: Graham's theatre background helped him improvise invisible patches.

questions.question: introduced a system to keep track of borrowed equipment?
questions.explanation: Priya created a numbered token system for shared tools.

questions.question: describes having to prevent an important local figure from receiving special treatment?
questions.explanation: Priya asked a local councillor to wait his turn.

questions.question: accepted the role only after being given a less stressful place to work?
questions.explanation: Leo agreed to volunteer after being promised a quiet corner.

questions.question: recalls a repair connected to a particularly personal public announcement?
questions.explanation: Leo repaired a radio that had broadcast the owner's wedding announcement.

questions.question: keeps written records because similar faults can appear again later?
questions.explanation: Leo keeps a notebook of unusual faults for future reference.

questions.question: defines success by the repaired objects being used in the room?
questions.explanation: Leo values hearing repaired radios playing on the refreshment table.
